person.name: Sir Piers "le Clerc" de Thornton, Lord of Thornton

nameForms.fullText: Sir Piers "le Clerc" de Thornton, Lord of Thornton

person.userCertifiedLifeSummary:
Biography
Earliest References: Charters of St. Werberg
James Tait has collected and analyzed the earliest charters pertaining to St. Weberg's Abbey in Chester. Tait notes that Peter first appears in the charters of St. Werberg, where he is identified as clerk to Ranulf III, Randle of Blundeville, Earl of Chester, who was in office 1181-1232. [1] Johannes and Thomas, clericus, appear with Peter between 1200 and 1208. [1]

"Petro clerico" the clerk personally wrote and witnessed (1200-1211) Ranulf III's confirmation of William de Venables' grant of half a salthouse at Northwick to St. Werberg's Abbye. (#326) (1200-1208) [2]
"Petrus cancellarius" once held rights in a toft in Aston upon Trent. Sir William Verdon later granted those rights to the abbey. [3]
Petrus clericus comitis Cestrie -- Peter, clerk of the Earl of Chester -- granted two oxgangs of land with their toftrs in the vill of Elton, and another toft in the midst of the vill and six lands in the fields, c. 1208-1228. [4] Elton was adjacent to Thornton le Moors, of which Peter beame Lord in the reign of John and after 1208. [5]
In his introduction to the charters, Tait provides further detail. Peter was the earl's clerk' aka clerk of Chester, Peter the Chancellor, lord of the manor of Thornton le Moors[1] Peter, the clerk of Ranulf III, earl of Chester, was sometimes styled clerk of Chester or Peter the Chancellor.[1] Ranulf de Blundeville was earl of Chester from 1181 to 1232. [6] Peter became lord of the manor of Thornton le Moors [grant was between October 1208 and April 1229].[1] He was sometimes called "clerk of Chester", and on two ocasions attested charters as "Peter the Chancellor." [1]

The relationship betwen the earl and his clerk was a close one. Ranulf stood godfather to Peter's son [7] and Peter became lord of the manor of Thornton le Moors and founder of the local family of Thornton (or Le Roter). [1]

Brother Robert

Peter had a brother named Robert.[8]

Ormerod
George Ormerod wrote in 1819 (extensively revised by Helsby in 1882) based on significant research on original documents. Ormerod notes that "As Peter the Clerk (Petro, clerico domini Comitis Cestriae, he was secretary to Randle Blundeville, the Earl of Chester. [9][10]

Thornton

During the reign of King John (1199-1216) the town of Thornton became a member of the fee of Aldford. During the period Richard de Aldford, lord of the fee, confirmed Thornton to Peter, secretary of the Earl of Chester. The charter by grant of Randle Blundeville, Earl of Chester, was executed by Philip de Orreby, justice of Chester, who was in office from 10 King John to 13th Henry III. (1208-1229). Thus the confirmation occurred between 1208 and 1216. [9]

Witnesses included Davide de Malopassu. [11]

Richard lord of Alford granted the township of Thornton-le

nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.
Indian: variant of   Dey  .

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Thornton,
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English and Scottish: habitational name from any of numerous places throughout England and Scotland so called, from Old English thorn ‘thorn tree’ + tūn ‘farmstead, estate’. The placename is most frequent in northern England, especially Yorkshire, where there are at least 16 possible sources for the surname.
Irish: Anglicized (translated) form of Gaelic Mac Sceacháin ‘son of Sceachán’ (see Skehan ).
Irish: shortened Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó Draighneáin ‘descendant of Draighneán’ (see Drennan ).

variants.name: Thornber
meanings: English: habitational name from any of the five minor places in Yorkshire called Thornber, particularly Thornber in Halton West. The placenames derive from Old English thorn ‘thorn’ + beorg ‘hill, mound’.
Irish: adopted for various Irish names containing an element meaning ‘thorn’; compare   Thornton  .

variants.name: Thorton
meanings: English: probably a shortened form of   Thornton  , or a habitational name from Thorton in Marnhull, Dorset, which has the same origin.

variants.name: Skehan
meanings: Irish: shortened Anglicized form of Gaelic Mac Sceacháin or Ó Sceacháin ‘son (or descendant) of Sceachán’, a personal name probably based on a diminutive of sceach ‘briar, thorn bush’. The Gaelic name has sometimes been ‘translated’ as Thornton .

nameMeanings.name: Piers
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Regular medieval vernacular form of Peter (from the Old French nominative case, as against the oblique Pier, modern Pierre). In the form Pierce it survived into the 18th century, although in part this may be a transferred use of the surname derived from the medieval given name. Piers was revived in the mid-20th century, perhaps partly under the influence of William Langland's great rambling medieval poem Piers Plowman ( 1367–86 ), in which the character of Piers symbolizes the virtues of hard work, honesty, and fairness.

nameMeanings.name: "le
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: 1  ♂ ‘Profit’ or ‘sharp’.
2  ♀, ♂ ‘Upright’.

nameMeanings.name: Clerc"
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Alain, Odette, Sebastien, Serge.

French: occupational or status name for a member of a minor religious order or for a scholar, Old French clerc, from Late Latin clericus, from Greek klērikos, a derivative of klēros ‘inheritance, legacy’, with reference to the priestly tribe of Levites (see Levy ) ‘whose inheritance was the Lord’. In medieval Christian Europe, clergy in minor orders were permitted to marry and so found families; thus the surname could become established. In the Middle Ages it was virtually only members of religious orders who learned to read and write, so that the term clerc later came to denote any literate man, a scholar (hence its modern meaning ‘scribe, secretary’ or ‘clerk’). Compare Leclerc .

variants.name: Clerge
meanings: French and Haitian (Clergé): nickname from French clergé ‘clergy’, a derivative of the Late Latin clericus (see Clerc ). This surname was brought to the US from Haiti, where it is most common, and also from the Dominican Republic, where it is of Haitian origin and rare.

variants.name: Clark
meanings: Transferred use of the surname, which originated as an occupational name denoting a clerk (Latin clericus), in the Middle Ages a man in minor holy orders who earned his living by his ability to read and write. It is recorded as a given name in Britain from the late 17th century onwards. Since the 1930s, when it was associated particularly with the film star Clark Gable ( 1901–60 ), it has been widely used as a given name, especially in North America.

variants.name: Levy
meanings: Variant spelling of   Lexie .

variants.name: De Klerk
meanings: Dutch: occupational name for a clerk or scribe, Middle Dutch clerc, from Late Latin clericus (see French Clerc ), with the definite article de. This surname is also established in South Africa, where it is reportedly (also) of French Huguenot origin (see 2 below). Compare De Clark and De Clerk .
Altered form of French Le Clercq (see Leclerc and Leclercq ) or of its Flemish variant De Clercq, cognates of 1 above, established in South Africa.

History: The progenitor of the (majority of the) South African bearers of the surname De Klerk of reportedly French origin (see 2 above) was Abraham le Clercq or de Clercq, born in Serooskerke in the Dutch province of Zeeland (where the Flemish surname De Clercq is also established), who arrived in the Dutch Cape Colony in 1688. His surname was later altered to de Klerk. He is recognized as a French Huguenot ancestor by the Huguenot Society of South Africa. One of his descendants is Frederik Willem de Klerk, president of South Africa from 1989 to 1994.

variants.name: Clerico
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Angelo, Carlo, Dino.
Italian: occupational or status name for a member of a minor religious order or for a scholar, from Late Latin clericus (see Clerc ).

en.lifeSummary: Sir Piers "le Clerc" de Thornton, Lord of Thornton was born in 1140, in Malpas, Cheshire, England. He married Cecelia Margaret Vaughn. They were the parents of at least 1 son. He died in 1202, at the age of 62.
